content.heading: Return Values — making your function answer a question

blocks.text: A function can do two things: it can log output so a human can read it, or it can return a value so the rest of your code can use it. The return keyword sends a value back to whoever called the function — and the function stops the moment it hits return.

blocks.desc: Send this value back to the caller — the function stops here
Call the function and capture whatever it returns into a variable

lines: // This function only logs — result disappears after it prints
// This function returns — the caller gets the value back
let answer = doubleReturn(5);   // answer is now 10
console.log('Triple is:', answer + 5);  // we can keep using it

blocks.label: return vs console.log
blocks.text: console.log shows a value on screen — but that value is gone the moment it prints. return hands the value back to your code so you can store it, do math with it, or pass it somewhere else. A function that only logs cannot be used in math or stored in a variable.

exercises.title: Write functions that return
tasks: Write a function called multiply that takes two numbers a and b and returns their product.
Store the result of calling multiply(3, 4) in a variable called product and log it.
Write a function called fullName that takes a first and last parameter and returns the combined name as one string with a space in between.
Call fullName with your own first and last name, store the result, and log it.
Call multiply three more times with different numbers and log each result.
exercises.hint: Use the return keyword inside the function body. The caller needs to store the result in a variable to use it: let result = multiply(3, 4);

exercises.title: Fix the missing return
tasks: Read the code carefully. The function double is supposed to give back a value, but something is wrong.
Predict: what will tripled log, and why?
Run it and read the output. Was your prediction right?
Fix the function so that calling double(5) returns 10, and tripled logs the correct number.
exercises.hint: console.log inside a function shows output but does not send anything back. The caller gets undefined instead. Replace the console.log with a return statement.
